draft.introduction: Federalist No. 1, written by Alexander Hamilton, opens the series of essays arguing for ratification of the Constitution. It frames the question in universal terms: whether societies can establish good government by reflection and choice, or are forever condemned to depend on accident and force.

translation.text: It has been frequently remarked that it seems to have been reserved to the people of this country, by their conduct and example, to decide the important question, whether societies of men are really capable or not of establishing good government from reflection and choice, or whether they are forever destined to depend for their political constitutions on accident and force.
translation.original_text: [Original English text as published in The Independent Journal, 1787]

translator_notes.note: Hamilton’s framing—reflection and choice versus accident and force—elevates the American constitutional project to a test case for the Enlightenment itself. The Federalist Papers collectively represent the most sophisticated application of political philosophy to practical constitutional design in history.
